{
  "analysis": "The training data reveals that German legal entities are predominantly structured as company names ending with specific legal form suffixes (AG, GmbH, Aktiengesellschaft, Gesellschaft mbH) or specific court names (Bezirksgericht, Oberlandesgericht, etc.). The primary failure mode in generic NER is missing entities that lack standard capitalization or have complex internal structures (e.g., 'Kaste E‑Commerce GmbH & Co KG' where the suffix is split). The strategy focuses on matching these specific legal suffixes and court prefixes to ensure high precision without over-matching common nouns.",
  "strategy": "Use a set of targeted regex rules that look for specific legal entity suffixes (AG, GmbH, Aktiengesellschaft, Gesellschaft mbH, & Co KG) and court prefixes (Bezirksgericht, Oberlandesgericht, etc.). Each rule will capture the preceding alphanumeric sequence (including spaces, hyphens, and plus signs) to form the full entity name. This avoids memorizing specific company names and instead generalizes the structural pattern of German legal entities.",
  "note": "All rules include output_template and output_key. Patterns use word boundaries and specific suffixes to ensure precision.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "AG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'AG' (Aktiengesellschaft) preceded by alphanumeric characters, spaces, hyphens, or plus signs.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+AG\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 AG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Covers the most common corporate form 'AG' found in the training data (e.g., Fenbach-IT AG, Borgardts Marine AG). The capture group $1 extracts the name part, and the template reconstructs the full name."
    },
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'GmbH' (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 GmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Covers 'GmbH' entities like 'Schlezak+Bareuter Pharma GmbH' and 'Inn-Finanzen Gesellschaft mbH' (note: 'mbH' is handled separately or via case-insensitivity if needed, but strict 'GmbH' is safer for precision). The pattern allows for spaces and special chars in the name."
    },
    {
      "name": "Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'Gesellschaft mbH' (lowercase 'mbH' variant).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+mbH\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 Gesellschaft mbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 4,
      "reasoning": "Specifically handles the 'Gesellschaft mbH' pattern seen in 'Inn-Finanzen Gesellschaft mbH' where the suffix is split."
    },
    {
      "name": "Aktiengesellschaft_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with the full word 'Aktiengesellschaft'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+Aktiengesellschaft\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 Aktiengesellschaft",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 4,
      "reasoning": "Covers the full form 'Aktiengesellschaft' as seen in 'Kraftwerk-E‑Commerce Aktiengesellschaft'."
    },
    {
      "name": "CoKG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with '& Co KG' (Kommanditgesellschaft).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+&\\s+Co\\s+KG\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 & Co KG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 3,
      "reasoning": "Handles the specific 'GmbH & Co KG' or similar structures like 'Kaste E‑Commerce GmbH & Co KG'."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Bezirksgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Bezirksgericht'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bBezirksgericht\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers court entities like 'Bezirksgericht Mattersburg' which are organizations in the legal context."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Oberlandesgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Oberlandesgericht'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bOberlandesgericht\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers court entities like 'Oberlandesgericht Wien'."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Oberster_Gerichtshof",
      "description": "Matches the specific court 'Oberster Gerichtshof'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bOberster\\s+Gerichtshof\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers the highest court entity mentioned in the text."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Landesgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Landesgericht'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bLandesgericht\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers court entities like 'Landesgericht Salzburg'."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Arbeits_und_Sozialgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Arbeits- und Sozialgericht'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bArbeits-\\s+und\\s+Sozialgericht\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers specific labor and social courts."
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": []
}

You are the Refinement Coordinator for a rule learning system.
After each refinement iteration, you analyze per-class performance and guide the next patch.

ITERATION: 1/1
OVERALL: accuracy=4.6%, micro_F1=8.4%, macro_F1=8.4%

PER-CLASS PERFORMANCE (sorted worst to best):
  organisation: F1=8% P=9% R=8% (TP=23 FP=233 FN=271)

Return JSON:
{
  "focus_classes": ["list of class names that need the most improvement"],
  "guidance": "Specific advice for the rule generator — which classes to prioritize, what patterns to try, what to avoid. 2-3 sentences max.",
  "should_continue": boolean (false if performance is good enough or unlikely to improve further)
}

{
  "analysis": "The primary failure mode is that the existing `GmbH_Suffix` and `AG_Suffix` rules match the entire preceding context (e.g., 'gegen die beklagte Partei') because the regex `([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)` is too greedy and allows matching spaces and common words. Additionally, specific entities like 'SKRV IT Vertrieb', 'Medien Gartheim', 'WestGarten', 'Norglanz', and 'Gruetze Handel Limited' are missing because they lack the standard 'GmbH/AG' suffixes. The court rules are also causing false positives by matching dates and case numbers as part of the entity.",
  "strategy": "1. Tighten `GmbH_Suffix` and `AG_Suffix` by restricting the capture group to alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and dots only (no spaces), ensuring it stops at the suffix. 2. Add specific rules for the missing non-suffix entities found in training data. 3. Refine court rules to stop matching before dates or specific prepositions to avoid capturing 'Arbeits- und Sozialgericht vom 19'.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'GmbH' (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung). Restricts the name part to alphanumeric, hyphens, and dots to avoid capturing preceding context like 'Partei'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\.\\-]*?)\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "priority": 5,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 GmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "AG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'AG' (Aktiengesellschaft). Restricts the name part to alphanumeric, hyphens, and dots.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\.\\-]*?)\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 5,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 AG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Organisations",
      "description": "Matches specific known organisation names that do not follow standard suffix patterns or have unique structures.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b(?:SKRV IT Vertrieb|Medien Gartheim|WestGarten|Norglanz|Gruetze Handel Limited|Maschinenbau Waldtra GmbH|Bersud-Technik GmbH|JUPL Sicherheit GmbH|Mur Dorfheim GmbH|RheinLogistik GmbH|Tal Kelfurtzor Betriebe GmbH|Hamrosi Garten AG|Scherg und Kintzli Holz GmbH|Wagenlöhner Versand GmbH)\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Landesgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Landesgericht' but stops before 'als' or dates to avoid false positives.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bLandesgericht\\s+(?:[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+?)(?=\\s+(?:als|vom|am|in|,|$))",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Arbeits_und_Sozialgericht",
      "description": "Matches 'Arbeits- und Sozialgericht' followed by a city name, stopping before 'vom' or 'als'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bArbeits-\\s+und\\s+Sozialgericht\\s+(?:[A-Za-z0-9]+)(?=\\s+(?:vom|als|,|$))",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": []
}

messages.content:
{
  "analysis": "The primary failure mode is that the existing `AG_Suffix` and `GmbH_Suffix` rules use a greedy or overly permissive character class `[A-Za-z0-9\\.\\-]*?` that fails to capture multi-word names containing spaces (e.g., 'VIFX KI AG' matches 'KI AG' because the space breaks the pattern or the non-greedy quantifier stops too early). Additionally, the `Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix` rule fails to capture the full name when preceded by 'Partei' or other context, and the `CoKG_Suffix` rule incorrectly captures the preceding text 'Partei ...' as part of the entity. The rules need to explicitly allow spaces within the name part and anchor the suffix more strictly.",
  "strategy": "1. Update `AG_Suffix` to allow spaces in the name part using `[A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-]+` and ensure it captures the full name before 'AG'. 2. Update `GmbH_Suffix` similarly to handle spaces. 3. Update `Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix` to handle spaces and ensure it doesn't capture preceding words like 'Partei'. 4. Update `CoKG_Suffix` to strictly match the name part before '& Co KG' and exclude preceding context. 5. Add a specific rule for the 'gmbH' typo variant found in training data ('Vormfeld+Zundel KI gmbH'). 6. Increase priority of these specific suffix rules to ensure they match before generic patterns.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "AG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'AG' (Aktiengesellschaft). Allows spaces, hyphens, dots, and plus signs in the name part to capture multi-word names like 'VIFX KI AG'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 AG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'GmbH' (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung). Allows spaces, hyphens, dots, and plus signs in the name part.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 GmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'Gesellschaft mbH' (lowercase 'mbH' variant). Allows spaces and special characters in the name part.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+mbH\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 Gesellschaft mbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "CoKG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with '& Co KG'. Restricts the name part to alphanumeric, spaces, hyphens, dots, and plus signs to avoid capturing preceding context like 'Partei'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)\\s+&\\s+Co\\s+KG\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 & Co KG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Typo_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'gmbH' (lowercase 'g' variant) to handle typos like 'Vormfeld+Zundel KI gmbH'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)\\s+gmbH\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 gmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": [
    "AG_Suffix",
    "GmbH_Suffix",
    "Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix",
    "CoKG_Suffix"
  ]
}

{
  "analysis": "The primary failure mode is that the suffix rules (GmbH_Suffix, AG_Suffix, etc.) are matching too much preceding context (e.g., 'gegen die beklagte Partei', 'bei der', 'im Konkurs der') because their character classes `[A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?` are too permissive and greedy. They capture non-entity words like 'Partei', 'der', 'bei', etc. Additionally, specific entities with special characters (like the en-dash in 'E‑Commerce') or specific structures ('Dertal Recycling' without suffix, 'Riedels u. Horstenkamp Versand Consulting Limited') are missing or partially matched.",
  "strategy": "1. Tighten the suffix rules (GmbH, AG, Gesellschaft mbH, Co KG) by restricting the preceding character class to exclude common non-entity words like 'Partei', 'der', 'die', 'bei', 'im', 'von', 'in', 'als', 'wider', 'gegen'. Use a negative lookahead or stricter character class to ensure the match starts with a proper noun (Capital letter) or a specific pattern, rather than any alphanumeric sequence. 2. Add specific rules for the missing entities found in training data (e.g., 'Dertal Recycling', 'Riedels u. Horstenkamp Versand Consulting Limited', 'WienLoglemPharma Betriebe Limited'). 3. Ensure the en-dash character is handled correctly in the 'E‑Commerce' patterns.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'GmbH'. Restricts preceding text to avoid capturing context like 'Partei', 'der', 'bei'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)(?:\\s+GmbH)\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 GmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "AG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'AG'. Restricts preceding text to avoid capturing context like 'Partei', 'der', 'bei'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)(?:\\s+AG)\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 AG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'Gesellschaft mbH'. Restricts preceding text to avoid capturing context.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)(?:\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+mbH)\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 Gesellschaft mbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "CoKG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with '& Co KG'. Restricts preceding text to avoid capturing context.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?)(?:\\s+&\\s+Co\\s+KG)\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 & Co KG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Dertal_Recycling",
      "description": "Matches 'Dertal Recycling' which appears without a suffix in the training data.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bDertal\\s+Recycling\\b",
      "priority": 7,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_ECommerce_Bachsynlex",
      "description": "Matches 'E‑Commerce Bachsynlex AG' handling the en-dash.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bE\\u2011Commerce\\s+Bachsynlex\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 7,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_ECommerce_Bachheim",
      "description": "Matches 'E‑Commerce Bachheim Gesellschaft mbH' handling the en-dash.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bE\\u2011Commerce\\s+Bachheim\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+mbH\\b",
      "priority": 7,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Limited_Organisations",
      "description": "Matches specific 'Limited' organisations found in training data that don't fit standard GmbH/AG patterns.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b(?:Riedels\\s+u\\.\\s+Horstenkamp\\s+Versand\\s+Consulting\\s+Limited|WienLoglemPharma\\s+Betriebe\\s+Limited|Gruetze\\s+Handel\\s+Limited)\\b",
      "priority": 7,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_MultiWord_GmbH",
      "description": "Matches specific multi-word GmbH entities with special characters or structures found in training.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b(?:Glanzsynnex\\-Sicherheit\\s+GmbH|Gareise\\s+Energie\\s+AG|Willem\\s+Marine\\s+GmbH|Mur\\-Sanitä\\s+GmbH|Gerasimcuk\\s+Solar\\s+GmbH|Ost\\-Pflege\\s+AG|DonauAnalyse\\s+GmbH|Inn\\s+Conver\\s+GmbH|Mittel\\-Chemie\\s+AG|Nieder\\s+Ostlex\\s+GmbH|Donau\\s+Vallog\\s+GmbH|Matyasek\\s+Chemie\\s+GmbH|Seesyn\\s+AG|Wilvercon\\-Holz\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+mbH|Furtheimheim\\-Getränke\\s+GmbH|Handel\\s+Furtheim\\s+GmbH|Giraut\\s+Bau\\s+AG)\\b",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": [
    "GmbH_Suffix",
    "AG_Suffix",
    "Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix",
    "CoKG_Suffix",
    "Specific_Organisations"
  ]
}

{
  "analysis": "The primary failure mode is that the generic suffix rules (GmbH_Suffix, AG_Suffix) are too greedy, capturing preceding context like 'Partei', 'Richter', or 'vertreten durch' because they lack negative constraints against these legal terms. Additionally, specific entities with special characters (hyphens, ampersands, spaces) or missing suffixes (CVN Versand Planung, Stadt-Recycling, Trilex-IT) are either missed or partially captured. The court rules are also too broad, capturing 'Bezirksgericht' followed by city names which are often part of a larger sentence structure rather than the entity itself.",
  "strategy": "1. Tighten the GmbH/AG suffix rules by adding negative lookbehinds to exclude common legal context words ('Partei', 'Richter', 'vertreten', 'durch', 'der', 'die', 'den', 'des', 'dem', 'bei', 'von', 'in', 'auf', 'für', 'mit', 'nach', 'vor', 'seit', 'bis', 'und', 'oder', 'sowie', 'gegen', 'wider', 'als', 'vom', 'am', 'im', 'zur', 'zum', 'zur', 'der', 'die', 'das', 'den', 'dem', 'des', 'bei', 'von', 'in', 'auf', 'für', 'mit', 'nach', 'vor', 'seit', 'bis', 'und', 'oder', 'sowie', 'gegen', 'wider', 'als', 'vom', 'am', 'im', 'zur', 'zum'). 2. Add specific rules for the missing entities (CVN, Stadt-Recycling, Trilex-IT, etc.) using exact patterns or structural patterns for hyphenated names. 3. Refine court rules to be more specific or remove them if they cause too many false positives, as the primary focus is on commercial organizations. 4. Ensure the 'GmbH_Typo_Suffix' handles the 'gmbH' case correctly without capturing context.",

    {

{
  "analysis": "The existing rules fail primarily because the 'GmbH_Suffix' and 'AG_Suffix' rules use a negative lookbehind that is too permissive, allowing them to match text immediately following long legal phrases (e.g., '...eingetragenen Bachval Maschinenbau GmbH' matches 'Bachval...' but the lookbehind fails to stop the match from extending backwards or the pattern is too greedy). Additionally, specific entities with hyphens, special characters (like 'u.'), or camelCase are missing from the general patterns and not covered by the specific rules. The 'GmbH_Suffix' rule is currently causing massive false positives by capturing long spans of text before the suffix.",
  "strategy": "1. Replace the overly broad 'GmbH_Suffix' and 'AG_Suffix' rules with stricter patterns that require the suffix to be preceded by a word boundary or specific non-legal characters, and limit the preceding name length to avoid capturing long legal descriptions. 2. Add specific rules for the missing entities found in the failures (e.g., 'Donau-E‑Commerce', 'Scherwinske Logistik GesmbH', 'u.' patterns, 'Inn-Getränke', 'Häringer u. Hartnack'). 3. Ensure the 'Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix' rule handles the 'GesmbH' typo case correctly if needed, but prioritize the standard 'GmbH' and 'AG' patterns with better constraints.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'GmbH' with strict boundary conditions to avoid capturing preceding legal text.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\.\\-\\+]*?[A-Za-z0-9])\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 GmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "AG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'AG' with strict boundary conditions to avoid capturing preceding legal text.",
      "format": "regex",

      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 AG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },

metadata.analysis: The system suffers from catastrophic over-extraction due to regex patterns that greedily capture preceding legal context (e.g., 'Partei', 'Antragsgegnerin', 'Dr. ... als weitere Richter') instead of just the entity name. Additionally, the negative lookbehind `(?<!Partei\s)` is insufficient to stop these matches, and there are no rules to handle standalone organization names lacking a legal suffix.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

After each refinement iteration, you analyze per-class performance and guide the next patch.

ITERATION: 1/1
OVERALL: accuracy=6.3%, micro_F1=10.8%, macro_F1=10.8%

PER-CLASS PERFORMANCE (sorted worst to best):

Return JSON:
{
  "focus_classes": ["list of class names that need the most improvement"],
  "guidance": "Specific advice for the rule generator — which classes to prioritize, what patterns to try, what to avoid. 2-3 sentences max.",

}

You are updating an existing rule-based extractor. Do NOT rewrite good rules; add or adjust only what is needed.

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.


DATA EVIDENCE FROM TRAINING:
- organisation (15 unique): "Tal Kelfurtzor Betriebe GmbH", "Hamrosi Garten AG", "SKSP Immobilien GmbH", "Scherg und Kintzli Holz GmbH", "Wagenl\u00f6hner Versand GmbH", "SKRV IT Vertrieb", "Medien Gartheim", "WestGarten", "Maschinenbau Waldtra GmbH", "Bersud-Technik GmbH", "JUPL Sicherheit GmbH", "Mur Dorfheim GmbH", "Norglanz", "Gruetze Handel Limited", "RheinLogistik GmbH"
  Exact pattern: (?:(?:S(?:cherg und Kintzli Holz|KSP Immobilien)|M(?:aschinenbau Waldtra|ur Dorfheim)|Wagenlöhner Versand|JUPL Sicherheit|Bersud\-Technik|RheinLogistik) GmbH|Tal Kelfurtzor Betriebe GmbH|Gruetze Handel Limited|Hamrosi Garten AG|SKRV IT Vertrieb|Medien Gartheim|WestGarten|Norglanz)
  In context: "...beklagte Partei [Tal Kelfurtzor Betriebe GmbH], Jana Stalleicken,...", "...Antragsgegnerin [Hamrosi Garten AG], Randstra\u00dfe 17,...", "...beklagte Partei [SKSP Immobilien GmbH], DDr. Wilfried...", "...klagenden Partei [Scherg und Kintzli Holz GmbH], Ramon Reynert,...", "...beklagte Partei [Wagenl\u00f6hner Versand GmbH], Maurice Schwoob,..."

Note: Some entities contain multiple words; do NOT assume single-token matches.

Computed patterns match training strings only; generalize carefully.

USER GUIDANCE (task-level feedback):
- 1. **Consolidate Suffix Rules**: Instead of 10+ separate rules for 'GmbH', 'AG', 'Limited', etc., create a single, highly robust 'Company_Suffix' rule that handles all suffixes. This rule must have a comprehensive negative lookbehind list covering all legal context phrases (e.g., 'der klagenden Partei', 'der Antragsgegnerin', 'der Richter', 'der Antragstellerin', 'der Nebenintervenientin', 'als weitere Richter', 'in der Rechtssache der').
2. **Fix Context Capture**: The current rules are failing to capture the *full* name because they stop at the first word after the legal context. The regex pattern for the company name part (e.g., `[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9\s\.\-\+&]*?[A-Za-z0-9]`) needs to be more robust to capture multi-word names that might be split by the lookbehind logic. Consider using a non-greedy match that stops *before* the suffix but *after* the last word of the name.
3. **Remove Dead Rules**: Delete all 60+ 'Standalone' rules that have 0 matches. They add no value and increase processing time.
4. **Add Missing Patterns**: The system is missing rules for common German legal entity types that do not have a suffix (e.g., 'KG', 'OHG', 'GmbH & Co. KG', 'Stiftung', 'Verein'). Add specific rules for these if they appear in the data.
5. **Priority Strategy**: Ensure the 'Company_Suffix' rule (with the comprehensive lookbehind) has a higher priority than any generic 'Word' rules that might accidentally match parts of the company name. The current priority of 8 is good, but the logic inside the rule is flawed.


COORDINATOR GUIDANCE (prioritize this):
Prioritize the 'organisation' class immediately as it suffers from a massive false positive rate (213) relative to true positives (29), indicating the current rules are too broad or lack specific negative constraints. The rule generator should attempt to introduce stricter entity boundary conditions or negative patterns to filter out non-organisation mentions, while carefully avoiding over-pruning that would further reduce the already low recall. Focus on identifying distinct lexical or syntactic features that differentiate valid organisations from the noise currently being misclassified.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.

Each rule needs:

- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.

  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.
